attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of joint pain, stiffness, and swelling affecting his fingers, toes, and lower back. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is swelling of multiple digits. His nails show pitting with separation from the nail bed. There is tenderness over the lumbar spine with reduced range of movement.

Investigations reveal C-reactive protein 25 mg/L (<5) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/hr (0-20). Plain radiographs of his hands and feet show erosions, new bone formation, and joint space narrowing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a combination of peripheral and axial arthritis, nail changes, and characteristic imaging findings most consistent with psoriatic arthritis. The key features supporting this diagnosis include the involvement of fingers and toes, nail pitting with onycholysis, lower back pain, and the distinctive radiographic pattern of both erosive changes and new bone formation. Psoriatic arthritis can occur without obvious skin psoriasis in approximately 15% of cases, with nail changes sometimes being the only extra-articular manifestation.

Axial spondyloarthritis primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints. While it can cause inflammatory back pain, the prominent peripheral joint involvement and nail changes seen in this case are not typical features. Additionally, the radiographic pattern of combined erosions and new bone formation in the peripheral joints is more characteristic of psoriatic arthritis.

Osteoarthritis typically affects weight-bearing joints and presents with pain that worsens with activity. It would not cause the inflammatory markers elevation seen here, nor would it present with nail changes. The patient's age and distribution of joint involvement also make inflammatory arthritis more likely than degenerative disease.

Rheumatoid arthritis characteristically causes symmetrical small joint polyarthritis with erosive changes on imaging. However, it does not cause nail changes or new bone formation. The involvement of the axial skeleton (apart from the cervical spine) is also uncommon in rheumatoid arthritis.

Systemic lupus erythematosus can cause non-erosive arthritis but would typically present with other systemic features such as rash, fatigue, or organ involvement. The erosive changes and new bone formation seen on imaging, along with the nail changes, are not consistent with lupus arthritis.
learningPoints: Psoriatic arthritis can present with peripheral arthritis, axial involvement, dactylitis, and characteristic nail changes even in the absence of obvious psoriasis.
Nail pitting and onycholysis are important clinical clues for psoriatic arthritis and may precede skin manifestations.
The combination of erosive arthritis with new bone formation on imaging is characteristic of psoriatic arthritis.
references: Ritchlin CT, Colbert RA, Gladman DD. Psoriatic Arthritis. N Engl J Med. 2017 Mar 9;376(10):957-970. doi: 10.1056/NEJMra1505557. Erratum in: N Engl J Med. 2017 May 25;376(21):2097. PMID: 28273019

Taylor W, Gladman D, Helliwell P, Marchesoni A, Mease P, Mielants H; CASPAR Study Group. Classification criteria for psoriatic arthritis: development of new criteria from a large international study. Arthritis Rheum. 2006 Aug;54(8):2665-73. doi: 10.1002/art.21972. PMID: 16871531.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
